Ice_Log: 1505 Sighted first ice floe.
1725 Passing through thin floe of ice. Maneuvering through small ice floes.
2109 Entered open water.. 2146 Commenced maneuvering on various north westerly courses through scattered ice floes.
2334 Entered open water.
1502 Ice increased in thickness. Fewer leads apparent.
4PM to 8PM Steaming as before on various courses and at various engine speeds through the ice.
2225 Commenced steering the base course through the ice.
0330 Commenced maneuvering on various northeasterly courses through scattered ice.
0415 Commenced maneuvering on various northeasterly courses through 90 percent coverage heavy ice.
1356 Launched Helicopter UP-4 with Captain as passenger to scout for leads in the ice.
1556 The ice is 1 1/2 to 2 feet thick with all leads frozen over to about 8 inches in thickness.
1129 An eskimo boarded the ship from the ice field the vessel being about 4 miles from Nome, Alaska.
1222 Commenced maneuvering to place ship in fast ice, Nome, Alaska.
1312: Moored in fast ice, starboard side to, with one deadman on the starboard, 7 1/2 fathoms
4PM to 8PM Moored in fast ice as before.
Mid to 4AM Moored starboard side to shore fast ice, Nome, Alaska
Mid to 4AM Moored starboard side to in fast ice with one eight inch line secured to dead man on ice, 7 1/2 fathoms of water, port of Nome, Alaska
0903 maneuvering through the ice on base course 285T and pgc.
1447 Commenced maneuvering in heavy ice.. 1457 Commenced flight operations for short range ice reconnaissance.
1834: Laying to 3 1/2 miles, bearing 097T, from King Island in a frozen lead.
8PM to Mid Drifting slowly with the ice pack in a southerly direction.
1016 All engines ahead on two main engines, 53 rpm, in order to hold ships nose into the ice Ukivok Village, King Island, due to difficult mooring.
Noon to 4PM Nosed into the ice as before.

1425 Secured ice anchor in shelf ice 50 yards ahead of ship.. 1435 All engines stopped. Ice anchor holding.. 1440 All engines ahead 30 rpm. Wire to ice anchor slipped.. 1447 All engines stopped. Ice anchor holding.
1502 All engines ahead 35 rpm. Ice anchor pulled out of ice as ship yawed.
4PM to 8PM Nosed into the ice as before.
2125 Steering casualty caused by ice in rudder. Repaired immediately.

Mid to 4AM This ship is fast in a hummock of ice. Efforts to dislodge the ship by use of the engines and the heeling system are of no avail.. 0047 Lying to in the ice using number 1 and 2 main engines to keep ice clear of the ship's stern. Drifting with the ice in a south easterly direction.
0820 Unable to free ship. Decided to free ship with explosives.. 0859 Launched UP-4 with the Navigator as passenger to scout ice for possible leads.

0905 UDT crew landed on the ice to place shaped charges on starboard beam.. 0913 Stopped all engines while UDT crew was on ice.. 0945 Set off blast with all six engines backing full. Ship would not come free.

1002 Widening crack in ice appeared ahead of ship. Ice in pressure ridge alongside to starboard began to crumble and ship came free.. 1028 Commenced maneuvering through leads making good a north easterly course.
1627 Launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance.
1716 Maneuvering on various courses through leads in the ice.. 1749 Ice thin and new. On base course 140T and pgc, having reversed course after running into heavy pack ice.
1830 Stopped all engines and took main engines 1 and 2 off the line and placed on 5 minutes notice. Lying to in new thin ice drifting approximately 170T and 2 knots.
2139 Encountered heavy ice 2 to 3 feet thick. Commenced maneuvering along the fringe of the ice.

0920 Placed the bow of the ship in shore fast ice in 31 feet of water approximately one mile from Wales, Alaska. 0920 Maintaining ship's position in the shore fast ice by going ahead on all engines 40 rpm.
1309 All engines stopped lying to in the ice.. 1336 All engines ahead at various speeds on various courses to maneuver through the ice.
2029 Lying to alongside the ice floe.

Mid to 4AM Operating in the Arctic ice pack off Cape Prince of Wales in accordance with ComServRonONE Op-Order 1-52. Lying to in the ice for the purpose of observing its set and drift with number 1 and 2 boilers, and number 3 auxiliary generator on the line.. 0035 Commenced docking practice on an ice floe.
0937 Put all main engines on the line and commenced moving through the ice on various courses.
Noon to 4PM Steaming as before through heavy rafted ice.
1456 Broke into a lead running in a southwesterly direction.
1545 Commenced maneuvering through heavy ice between leads.
0846 Launched UP-7 to make ice reconnaisence
1326 Launched helicopter UP-7 for an ice reconnaissance.
1535 Stopped - wedged in a ridge of pressure ice.
4PM to 8PM Wedged in pressure ice as before. Using various speeds to  free the ship.
8PM to Mid Wedged in pressure ice as before.. 2030 Commenced using all engines at various speeds to work free of ice.
0714 Commenced putting main engines on the line and making turns to clear ice from astern.. 0729 Six main engines on the line. Attempting to break free from the ice.
0812 UDT personnel disembarked to port for the purpose of placing shaped charges to blast the ship free of the ice.. 0842 First blast set off. Ship backed free of the ice.
1124 Ship fast in pressure ridge.
1205 With forward trim tank full of water and after trim tank empty, ship fishtailed out of ice.
8PM to Mid Steaming on southeasterly courses through snow covered pack ice.
2304 The ship is fast in the ice.. 2349 The ship is free of the ice.
0555 beset in heavy ice.
0713 free of ice.
0813 Launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance, pilot LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615 USN, with CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694 USN as passenger.
0920 entered long lead.. 0948 Launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance, pilot LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615 USN with LT J.K. ATHOW, 323411 USN as passenger.
1125 steady on base course 160T and pgc, 149 psc, 151 pstgc, having reached new ice approximately 6 inches thick broken with numerous small leads.
Noon to 4PM Steaming as before through ice 1 to 2 feet thick with light snow cover.
4PM to 8PM Ice conditions, 1 foot to 18 inches thick, light snow cover.
8PM to Mid During this watch passage was through brash ice and broken floes of medium ice.
0345 the ship is fast in heavy ice.
4AM to 8AM Fast in heavy ice as before. . 4AM to 8AM Ice conditions medium to heavy, 1 to 2 feet ice.
0619 ship backed free of heavy ice.
0915 Launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance. 0937 ice conditions - numerous leads in medium ice. 0937 Medium ice with no leads.
1058 launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance. 1058 They observed a hulk beset in ice 1 miles south of Sledge Island.
1352 launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance
1440 Nosed into the shore fast ice 3/4 miles off Nome, Alaska.
4PM to 8PM Nosed into fast ice as before.
1745 commenced backing out of shelf ice one mile from Nome, Alaska.
1802 Ice ahead 12 to 20 inches thick with 4 inch snow coverage.
8PM to Mid Ice conditions - heavy pack ice with numerous pressure ridges.
2228 beset in heavy pressure ridge.
Mid to 4AM Beset in heavy ice 17 miles 215T from Nome, Alaska drifting in a northwesterly direction with the ice.
4AM to 8AM Beset as before.
0742 Commenced maneuvering at various speeds to clear brash ice from the stern of the ship.

8AM to Noon Beset in ice as before.. 0805 launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance mission. 0814 ice wedged to port was blasted by UDT-3. Failed to free ship from ice.. 0855 with all engines backing full, ship backed free of ice.. 8AM to Noon Ice conditions medium with ice 1 1/2 to 2 1/2 feet and 6 inch snow cover.

Noon to 4PM Steaming as before in heavy pack ice with no leads.. 1228 commenced flight operations. Launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance. 1257 recovered helicopter UP-7. Covered area 3-5 miles ahead of the ship

1418  launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance. 1455 recovered helicopter UP-7. Area covered 5 miles on 170T to 190T. Expended 1/3 tank of fuel, refueling immediately upon return. Ice reconnaissance produced fair leads in very heavy ice.
4PM to 8PM heavy pack ice with numerous shot leads.. 1645  Launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance
1700 recovered helicopter UP-7. Expended 1/4 tank of fuel, refeuling immediately upon return. Covered area 3-5 miles ahead of ship on bearing 225T.
1820 entered long lead going southwest. . 1846 end of lead after 4 1/2 miles.

8PM to Mid 90% coverage by floe ice averaging 24 inches in thickness with 6 inch snow cover. Ice not under pressure, but tough and resistant. 10% open leads, short and not continuous. Apparent ice movement west north west, estimated speed 1 1/2 knots. . 8PM to Mid Reverse of trim considerably aids engines in backing off heavy ice.
0513 became stuck in ice hummock.. 0555 stopped all engines, manipulations of engine speeds having failed to free the ship from ice.
0747 Commenced maneuvering to free ship from ice. . 0756 ship freed from ice.

0801 launched helicopter UP-7 with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1 USN as pilot and CDR. E.H. MAHER, 71694 USN as ice observer on an ice reconnaissance 3 to 5 miles ahead of the ship.. 0824 Steaming through ice 18 to 30 inches thick with a snow cover. The ice covers over 90% of the visible area.
1050 Launched helicopter UP-7 with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615, USN as pilot and LT J.K. ATHOW, 323411, USN as ice observer on an ice reconnaissance up to 10 miles ahead of the ship.
1230 entered area of 100% coverage, 18 inch ice with 4 inches of snow. Few small pressure ridges. No leads visible in ice.

1445 due to pressure, ice closes over ships wake immediately. Occasionally impossible to back. . 1455 commenced steering courses 45 either side of base course. Wake remains clear of ice and progress is much easier.

1636 Launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance. 1655 recovered helicopter UP-7. Re-fueld immediately having expended 1/4 tank of fuel. Reconnaissance 6 1/2 miles ahead of the ship.. 4PM to 8PM Heavy consolidated pack ice, 100% coverage, 2 feet thick with 1 foot of snow.
8PM to Mid Ice 2 1/2 feet thick and very tough with 1 foot of snow drifting and obscuring pressure ridges. Much backing down required to negotiate pressure ridges.
0125 sighted 1 white arctic fox off port bow. Fox ran away from cargo lights and into darkness when ship was stopped by an ice floe.
4AM to 8AM Ice conditions - heavy ice with ridges and hummocks.
0750 launched helicopter UP-7 on an ice reconnaissance with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615, USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as observer.
0812 This reconnaissance covered and area approximately 5 miles ahead of the ship on bearings from 215T to 260T.. 0828 stopped - lying to in our own cleared track.
0927 numbers 1 and 2 engines ahead slow to keep ice from compacting around the stern.
1245 Commenced maneuvering on various south westerly courses and at various speeds in heavy snow covered ice. The wake of the ship is full of brash ice.

1311 launched helicopter UP-7 on an ice reconnaissance with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615, USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as ice observer.  Reconnaissance covered up to 7 miles from the ship.
4PM to 8PM Lying to in ice as before.
8PM to Mid Lying to in ice as before.
2100 four men on ice constructing igloo.
4AM to 8AM No changes in ice conditions.

0801 launched helicopter UP-7 with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1 USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694 USN as passenger on an ice reconnaissance flight.. 0831 recovered helicopter UP-7. No change in ice conditions.

1246 launched helicopter UP-7 with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1 USN as pilot and ANDERSON, W.L., 30200 62, PH3 USN as passenger for possible mail pickup from ice. . 1246 helicopter UP-7 landed on ice.
1300 mail recovered and brought on board. Navy 9347 made a short ice reconnaissence to the southward for the ship and at 1325 left this area to continue its routine flight.
4PM to 8PM No change in ice conditions.
0-4 lying to in consolidated pack ice 5 feet thick with drifted snow cover 25 miles northeast of Northeast Cape, St. Lawrence Island.
0700 noted a north-south lead ahead of the ship which had opened during the night.

0837 launched helicopter UP-7 with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615 USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as ice reconaissance observer.. 0848 underway on various courses and speeds through leads, brash and broken floes in a southwesterly direction.
0927 recovered helicopter UP-7 having expended 1/2 tank of gas and ranged 14 miles ahead of ship
1109 launched helicopter UP-7 with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1 USN as pilot and LT G.F. CHANDLER, 98800 USNR as ice observer.

Noon to 4PM: Course made good 201T, distance 25.8 miles through brash ice and broken leads. 1253 launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORN III, 521615, USN as pilot and ENS E.J. CONDON, 511625 USN as passenger.
1513 launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26 , AD1 USN as pilot and LT J.K. ATHOW, 323411 USN as observer.
1652 launched helicopter UP-7 for an ice reconnaissance with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1 USN as pilot and CDR. E.H. MAHER, 71694 USN as observer.
1800 ran out of leads and into pack ice of about 4 feet thick with no snow cover. 90% of the general area is covered by ice.
2040 commenced maneuvering to northeast to return to lead.
2139 back in leads.

2200 ran out of leads into pack ice. . 2214 lying to. Secured main generators numbered 5 and 6 on five minutes notice. Secured main generators number 1,2,3 and 4 on 30 minutes notice. Secured heeling. Returning to the lead.
0742 Commenced flight operations. Launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615, USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as observer.
0818 Set base course 200T, maneuvering between ice floes.
0932 started heeling to free ship from two floes. . 0940 free from floes. Secured heeling.
1253 launched helicopter UP-7 on an ice reconnaissance with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615, USN as pilot and LT J.K. ATHOW, 323021, USN as observer.

1600 Ice conditions varying from 24 inches thick to open leads.. 1651 launched helicopter UP-7 on an ice reconnaissance flight with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1, USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as observer.
0140 Underway on base course 210T. Maneuvering on various courses and at various speeds through a south southwesterly lead.
0220 ran out of leads into snow covered pack ice about 2 feet thick.
0635 placed main generators number 1 and 2 on the line and steamed ahead to clear propellers of ice.
0711 Commenced maneuvering through ice on base course 210T and pgc.
8AM to Noon Consolidated heavy pack, 3 to 4 feet thick with snow cover and very few leads.
1059 launched helicopter UP-7 for ice reconnaissance with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1, USN as pilot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as observer.

1505 launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance with LTJG J.R. WILLIFORD III, 521615, USN as polot and CDR E.H. MAHER, 71694, USN as observer.. 1540 ice has become thinner consisting of broken floes 1 foot thick with 6 inches snow and small patches of broken water.

4PM to 8PM: Distance made good 25.5 miles on course 205T at 6.5 knots through ice fields of horizon to horizon coverage, averaging 2 feet in thickness with a 4 to 10 inch drifted snow cover. 1659 launched helicopter UP-7 on ice reconnaissance with VANDIVER, L.H., 376 41 26, AD1, USN as pilot and LT. J.K. ATHOW, 323021, USN as observer.. 4PM to 8PM Open water in cracks and narrow leads striking 140T averaging less than 1% in area covered. Ice is hard with few mild pressure ridges and no rafting. Ten inches snow cover on two feet of ice sufficient to stop headway on present power conditions.
8PM to Mid Steaming as before. Base course 210T, through broken floes and leads.
0430 Open water. Commenced steaming through broken up ice floes with large areas of open water between them. The ice is 8 to 10 inches thick. At places new ice is forming between pancakes.
0840 fewer ice floes encountered. Ice floes in very small pieces.
Noon to 4PM No ice encountered.

1833: Ice is scattered and 1 to 5 feet thick.
8PM to Mid: Following the edge of the ice pack in a south-westerly direction. The apparent drift of the ice is in a southerly direction. with a tendency to break up and disintegrate.
00 to 04: Conducting ice reconnaissance west and south from Point Barrow. Steering various courses to skirt ice pack in the general direction of 255 T.
0300: Ice beyond radar range, moving northeast.
2326: Contacted scattered light floe ice at an estimated distance of 7 miles, bearing 260 T. Heavier floes of broken ice beyond light floes.
1514: Changed course to 075 true gyro, 052 psc skirting ice pack headed in a easterly direction.
1600-1800: Continuing to skirt ice pack. Ice in approximately same position as earlier today.
1709: Changed course to investigate ice closer toward shore.
2353: started maneuvering on various easterly courses skirting the edge of the ice.
0053: commenced maneuvering on various courses while navigating through broken ice pack.
192: proceeded through scattered ice at 10 knots. Skirting the ice pack in a manner so as to delineate its limits.
1138: Ice conditions - light and scattered floes and brash.
1600-1800: Commenced maneuvering on various courses on ice reconnaissance.
1857: commenced maneuvering through broken ice on various courses at various speeds.
20 to 24: Making good a northeasterly course for Point Barrow, skirting and plotting ice pack.
00 to 04: Steaming on various courses and speeds enroute from Point Barrow, Alaska on ice reconnaissance.
0521: commenced maneuvering on various soth-westerly courses following the edge of the loose pack ice.
2247: commenced maneuvering on various courses to plot ice.
0303: The ice appears to be settingin a north-easterly direction.
0410: making good a northeasterly course scouting ice pack toward Point Barrow.
0636: making good a course of 235 T, scouting ice pack. 0658: Ice pack appears to be moving in a northerly direction at between 1 and 2 knots.
0712: Steaming in open water.
1730: commenced maneuvering on various courses and speeds while passing thru close pack ice.
1948: commenced following the edge of the ice to the south west.

Mid to 4AM: Steaming at 10 knots (80 rmp) skirting ice to outline its limits. 0025: at a point opposite the beaches of Point Barrow changed course to 225 pgc, 227 T and thenceforth steered various courses in a south-westerly direction folloiwing the ice pack.  0303 The ice appears to be settingin a north-easterly direction.
0900: changed course to 040 pgc, having determined the extreme southern limit of the ice to be 11 miles from the beach; proceeding to Point Barrow to fix position of ice floe off Point Barrow.
1119: commenced steering various courses in the ice.
1905: maneuvering on various northeasterly courses scouting edge of loose ice.
2345: commenced steaming as icebreaker guard for ComBarex ships.
1039: maneuvering on various courses and speeds to check drift of ice.
1132: completed check of ice drift.
1202: maneuvering on various courses north and south to check drift of ice. Drift 35, set 2.0 knots.
1344: Scattered ice 8 miles north of Point Barrow.
1440: observed ice floes near the U.S.S. Taluga.

0222: approached anchorage "George", reversed course. 0254 reversed course at edge of ice.
324: reversed course at anchorage "George". 0351 reversed course at edge of ice.
0440: commenced maneuvering on various courses to avoid ice.
0551: commenced maneuvering on various courses and speeds to avoid ice floes.
2330: proceeding at 6 knots on various courses through the ice.

1645: Maneuvering through scattered ice floes.
1823: commenced maneuvering at various speeds on base course 107 T to steer through leads in the ice. Ice conditions: scattered and broken floes and brash.
8PM to Mid: Maneuvering on various courses and speeds to proceed through broken floes and brash to make good course 107 T.
2203: Ice conditions are broken ice with .5 to .7 coverage.
0630: maneuvering through heavy ice.

16 to 18: Steaming on various courses through heavy ice floes attempting to make good course of 115 t.  18 to 20 Steaming on various courses through heavy ice floes attempting to make good course of 115 t.

18 to 20: Steaming on various courses through heavy ice floes attempting to make good course of 115 t.

Mid to 4AM: Escorting LSTs through ice.
0324: Commenced maneuvering at various speeds on various courses to maneuver through the ice. Ice conditions: moderately heavy floes rotting with less than half coverage.
4AM to 8AM: Steaming as before on various courses and speeds to maneuver through ice. Ice conditions: moderately heavy broken ice, rotting. Thin ice being frozen on surface of water.
Mid to 4AM: enroute Barter Island from Pitt Point, Alaska on various courses at various speeds, to maneuver through the ice pack.
0413: Commenced maneuvering on various easterly courses and at various speeds to move clear of heavy ice and shoal waters.
0707: Commenced maneuvering through the ice. Ice conditions: scattered and broken floes, ice rotten with 1/4 coverage.
0800: Maneuvering as before on various courses and speeds to maneuver through ice to make good course 272 T and 270 pgc.
1200: Ice conditions: scattered small floes, block and brash ice with .3 coverage.

8AM to Noon: Steaming as before through broken ice with approximately five tenths coverage on a base course of 228 T, and 286 pgc.  . 8AM to Noon: Throughout this watch ice conditions improved. Ice coverage at 1200 is less than one tenth.
Noon to 4PM: Ice conditions: open water  with less than one tenth coverage.
4PM to 6PM: Ice conditions: open water.
1950: Entered scattered ice.

0500: commenced maneuvering through scattered floes of ice.
0500: commenced maneuvering through scattered floes of ice.

0225: changed course to 272 T and 270 pgc having cleared ice pack to the northwest.

2011: slowed to enter an ice field.
0308: commenced maneuvering on various courses and speeds to avoid large ice floe.
1010: maneuvering at various courses and speeds around ice floe.
1324: Maneuvering through light ice floes at various speeds.
1400: entered field of ice with 60 percent of puddling. Puddles not burned through. Coverage is 90 per cent.
1540: Underway. At various speeds through puddled ice.
4PM to 6PM: Maneuvering though the ice as before.
6PM to 8PM: Maneuvering as before to proceed through ice.
Noon to 4PM: Lying to in 90% coverage of light ice floes.
Mid to 4AM: Laying to and ice coverage about five-tenths of the surface.
0725: Underway on various courses to remain in area to free submarine from the ice.

1121: helicopter UP-4 made forced landing on ice due to broken fan belt, bearing 082o distance 3.5 miles. 1143: maneuvering on various courses and speeds to proceed toward helicopter UP-4. Ice conditions moderate heavy winter ice with 50 percent puddling.
1756: Laying to in a polynya.

Noon to 4PM: Ice conditions: light with large open water areas.
1529: Ice reconnaissance plane made an air drop on ice floe adjacent ship.
1710: commenced maneuvering to maintian instruments free of ice.
Mid to 4AM: Lying to in large open lead.
1800: maneuvered to maintain test equipment free of ice.

0000: UP-7 landed on the ice due to a broken fan belt and the ships course was immediately changed to the vicinity of UP-7.
Mid to 4AM: Ice condition: moderate heavy ice with surface pools, coverage to 80 percent.
0100: Entered an area of small to large floes of winter ice. Coverage is 0.3.
0245: the ice conditions changed to small to medium floes of rotten ice. Coverage is 0.4.
0315: Ice coverage decreasing.
4AM to 8AM: Steaming as before in rotten ice. Ice conditions: moderately heavy broken ice with about 0.4 coverage.
8AM to Noon: Steaming as before in rotten ice.

1200: Steaming as before conducting docking exercises to ice floes.
1622: commenced maneuvering to place port side to the ice.
0000: Condition of the ice: broken floes with .6 coverage.

1830: maneuvering on various courses to make good base course and avoid ice. Ice conditions: twenty percent coverage, small floes, block and brash ice.
8PM to Mid: Steaming as before in scattered ice of 35 percent coverage.
Mid to 4AM: Steaming on various courses and speeds to avoid heavy ice.
0223: maneuvering to avoid ice.
0412: commenced maneuverng on various courses to avoid ice.
1651: sighted ice floes ahead of ship bering 066 gyro, distance 5 miles.

0232: encountered scattered block ice with some giant floes. Coverage 0.2.
0730: commenced maneuvering on various courses to avoild ice along base course. 0730 resumed steering course 74 T. .
